headline: Valentine's Day Shootout at Stoneville, 1884
body:
The wind bit sharp across the Little Missouri, a cold that seeped into bone and sinew. It was February 14, 1884, and Stoneville, a speck of a town in Montana Territory, was about to earn its grim place in the annals of frontier violence. George Axelby, a man whose mouth was as quick as his trigger finger, had made a boast. His two-bit horse-thieving pal, Jesse Pruden, was jailed in Miles City, bound for Spearfish, Dakota Territory. Axelby swore he’d spring him, even if it meant fighting to the last man. Such words, spoken loud in a land where law was often a whisper against the howl of the wild, reached the ears of Deputy U.S. Marshal A.M. Willard. Willard, a man forged on the Great Lakes, knew the breed. He knew Axelby was dangerous, capable of the kind of reckless stupidity that left bodies in its wake.

Willard gathered his posse in Spearfish: his brother Fred, Deputies John “Jack” O’Harra, John Duffy, Frank Jackson, and a cowboy named McNarboe. Their mission: intercept Axelby before his foolish bravado turned bloody. Fate, a cruel mistress on the frontier, brought them to Stoneville. The town, a telegraph relay station and stagecoach stop, boasted Lew Stone’s saloon, the only beacon of warmth and whiskey for miles. By noon, Willard and McNarboe were in Stone’s house, learning Axelby and his gang were just 250 yards away, drowning their nerves in cheap liquor. The lawmen waited, silent as the falling snow, for the outlaws to make their move.

Just after three that afternoon, the saloon doors swung open. Axelby and his five associates stumbled out, drunk, pistols barking at the indifferent sky. The Willard posse erupted from Stone’s house, scattering like quail, taking cover as the gang scrambled onto their horses. Deputy Fred Willard and the outlaw Jack Campbell fired almost as one. Billy the Kid McCarthy, the last man out, hadn’t even mounted. He snatched his Winchester, dropped behind a fence, and returned fire. The air filled with the crack of rifles, the thud of hooves, the shouts of men. It was a symphony of lead and desperation.

Deputy O’Harra, a man of courage, got off eight or nine rounds before a bullet from McCarthy’s Winchester tore through his left side. He fell, bleeding, but his spirit was unbroken. “Fred, I came out with you to stand by you, and I have staid as long as I could; give them the best you have,” he gasped, his words a testament to the brutal loyalty of the frontier. Two men dragged him back to Stone’s house, but within ten minutes, the life had drained from him. Fred Willard took a bullet between the shoulders, two more ripped his clothes, and A.M. Willard felt lead crease his ear and tear his coat. The lawmen were taking a beating.

The outlaws fared no better. Fred Willard’s first shot grazed Jack Campbell’s head, sending him sprawling as his horse collapsed, shot dead. Campbell, dazed, crawled into the brush, seeking the cold embrace of the earth. Axelby himself took a rifle slug in the thigh, his horse shot from under him. He limped into the bushes, bullets whistling past, a wounded animal seeking refuge. Harry Tuttle, riding a mule, found himself in an impromptu rodeo as his terrified mount bucked and spun. A posse member’s bullet shattered his left elbow, sending him to the ground, clutching his arm, seeking the same desperate cover as his comrades. The foresight of Fred Willard, shooting the horses, had grounded the gang, forcing them to flee on foot. As Axelby vanished, he fired parting shots, one of which struck William Cunningham, a cook from the Driscoll ranch, who had stepped out to witness the carnage. Cunningham fell, dead. Another cowboy, Jack Harris, also caught a bullet, dying later of his wounds. The shootout had claimed three lives, two of them innocent bystanders.

As darkness fell and snow began to drift, the posse tended to their wounded and mourned their dead. Axelby and his men had escaped, but the trail would be fresh in the morning. The killing of Cunningham and Harris ignited a fierce thirst for vengeance among the local cowboys, many of whom joined the posse. On February 16, A.M. Willard and his expanded force met Deputy Ryan, who was bringing Pruden to Spearfish. The outlaws, horseless and wounded, were in no condition to attempt a rescue. Reinforcements poured into Stoneville, and the hunt continued.

Harry Tuttle, his elbow throbbing, didn’t get far. He found temporary refuge at the Shuster ranch, where a cowboy tourniqueted his arm so tightly it caused more agony than the wound itself. The posse soon found him, and Willard sent him to the hospital in Spearfish. Jack Campbell, meanwhile, followed the Little Missouri downstream to the James Walker place. From there, he penned a desperate note to a ranch foreman named Hood at Stoneville Saloon: “Dear Hood: I was badly wounded in the head during the fight yesterday, and my horse was killed. The boys were all shot to pieces and scattered. For God’s sake, send me a horse by bearer as soon as it is dark enough to get away from the officers.” The local mail carrier, Billie Chase, delivered the note, but Willard’s men intercepted it. They instructed Chase to take a horse to the Walker place, tie it to the corral, and tell Campbell to leave at once. What followed was a grim testament to frontier justice.

Years later, eyewitness Henry Walker claimed the posse arrived at the ranch in the dead of night, shooting Campbell without warning as he emerged. Walker swore Campbell begged to write a note to his mother, a plea denied before each posse member emptied his gun into the dying man. Campbell’s frozen body, Walker said, lay in the yard for two days before being hauled to town, used as a seat on a lumber wagon. The sheriff’s office, however, offered a different tale. The February 19 Daily Yellowstone Journal reported that when Campbell appeared, a lawman cried, “Throw up your hands!” Campbell, defiant, whirled and fired a single shot before the posse “perforated with bullets” the dangerous outlaw. The Black Hills Times, on February 20, added that Campbell, after crawling from the river, had been tracked to the Walker ranch. The truth, like so many tales of the wild west, was buried in the dust and blood of that Valentine’s Day, a day when the law, or what passed for it, met the lawless with brutal finality.
